questions.question: Alexander the Great built one of history's largest empires by age 32. He was tutored by Aristotle and came from which ancient kingdom adjacent to northern Greece?
questions.explanation: Alexander the Great (356-323 BC) was born in Pella, the capital of the ancient kingdom of Macedon. His father, Philip II, had already made Macedon the dominant power in Greece. Alexander went on to conquer Persia, Egypt, and parts of India, spreading Greek culture across a vast empire before dying at age 32 in Babylon.
questions.memory_hook: Alexander the Great: Macedon, tutored by Aristotle, conquered to India by 32.

questions.question: The battles of Marathon, Thermopylae, and Salamis — fought between 490 and 480 BC — were all part of conflicts between Greek city-states and which ancient empire?
questions.explanation: The Greco-Persian Wars pitted Greek city-states against the Achaemenid Persian Empire. The Battle of Marathon (490 BC), the famous 300 Spartans at Thermopylae (480 BC), and the naval victory at Salamis (480 BC) were key engagements. Greece ultimately repelled the invasions.
questions.memory_hook: Marathon + Thermopylae + Salamis = Greece vs. Persia. The 300 Spartans held Thermopylae.

questions.question: Which country dropped atomic bombs on Japan in 1945?
questions.explanation: The United States dropped atomic bombs on Hiroshima (August 6, 1945) and Nagasaki (August 9, 1945) — the only wartime use of nuclear weapons in history. The bombs killed an estimated 110,000-210,000 people, most of them civilians. Japan announced its surrender on August 15, 1945. The bombings remain controversial, debated between those who argue they ended the war quickly and those who see them as war crimes.
questions.memory_hook: USA dropped the atomic bombs on Japan in 1945. Two cities, three days apart. The only nuclear weapons ever used in warfare. The decision is still debated 80 years later. Japan surrendered 6 days after Nagasaki.

questions.question: In which sport does a player score by hitting the ball with a stick into a goal, played on horseback?
questions.explanation: Polo is played on horseback, with each team of four trying to score goals by hitting a small ball with a long-handled mallet. It is one of the world's oldest team sports, originating in Persia around 500 BC. The sport spread through Asia and was adopted by British cavalry officers in India in the 19th century. Today it is closely associated with high society in Britain, Argentina, and the US.
questions.memory_hook: Polo = horseback + stick + ball + goal. One of the oldest team sports (500 BC Persia). British cavalry officers learned it in India. The playing field is 300 yards long — about 3 football fields. Horses are changed every two periods (chukkers) because the pace exhausts them.

questions.question: How many gold medals did Michael Phelps win at the 2008 Beijing Olympics?
questions.explanation: Michael Phelps won 8 gold medals at the 2008 Beijing Olympics, surpassing Mark Spitz's record of 7 golds at a single Olympics (1972 Munich). Phelps competed in 8 events and won all 8. One of the most dramatic was the 100m butterfly, where he won by 0.01 seconds after a controversial wall touch by his competitor. His 8 golds in one Games remains an Olympic record.
questions.memory_hook: Phelps = 8 golds in Beijing 2008. Beat Spitz's 36-year-old record. Won the 100m butterfly by 0.01 seconds in one of the most dramatic races in Olympic history. He competed twice a day for over a week and ate 12,000 calories daily — partly just to fuel the training.

questions.question: Which rapper's 2003 album 'The College Dropout' launched a career that would make him one of music's most influential artists?
questions.explanation: Kanye West released The College Dropout in February 2004, defying industry expectations that rappers had to be 'street' to succeed. The album featured soul-sampled production, introspective lyrics, and skits. It debuted at number 2 on the Billboard 200 and won Best Rap Album at the Grammys. Kanye went on to revolutionize hip-hop production across several more critically acclaimed albums.
questions.memory_hook: Kanye West = The College Dropout. A rapper who was rejected by labels because he wasn't 'street' enough. Made an album about not fitting the rap mold and became one of the most influential artists in music history. His impact on production, fashion, and cultural conversation is unmatched in his generation.

questions.question: The film 'La La Land' (2016) was directed by which filmmaker and starred Emma Stone and Ryan Gosling?
questions.explanation: Damien Chazelle directed La La Land (2016), a modern musical about two artists pursuing their dreams in Los Angeles. The film won 6 Academy Awards, including Best Director (Chazelle, who became the youngest Best Director winner at 32) and Best Actress (Emma Stone). At the Oscars, it was erroneously announced as Best Picture before the correct winner (Moonlight) was revealed — the most dramatic Oscar mistake ever.
questions.memory_hook: La La Land = Damien Chazelle, 2016. Youngest Best Director winner at 32. Won 6 Oscars. Then the Best Picture envelope was wrong — La La Land announced, then Moonlight corrected. The most dramatic Oscars moment in history wasn't a speech, it was a mistake.

questions.question: Which rapper's album 'good kid, m.A.A.d city' (2012) is widely considered one of the greatest rap albums ever?
questions.explanation: Kendrick Lamar's major-label debut good kid, m.A.A.d city was released in October 2012 and has sold over 5 million copies. The album tells the story of a day in Compton from Lamar's teenage perspective, functioning as a short film about growing up surrounded by gang culture. It is widely considered the greatest rap album of the 2010s and helped establish Lamar as his generation's most important hip-hop artist.
questions.memory_hook: Kendrick Lamar = good kid, m.A.A.d city. A concept album about one day in Compton. It functions like a movie — scene by scene, character by character. It's the reason Kendrick eventually won the Pulitzer Prize for Music. Hip-hop as literature, recognized by the literary establishment.

questions.question: Which two continents are separated by the Ural Mountains and Ural River?
questions.explanation: The Ural Mountains and Ural River form the traditional boundary between Europe and Asia. Russia straddles both continents — west of the Urals is European Russia (including Moscow), east of the Urals is Asian Russia (Siberia). This boundary means that Russia is technically partly in both continents, and cities like Yekaterinburg straddle the continental divide.
questions.memory_hook: Urals = Europe meets Asia. The boundary runs through the middle of Russia. Moscow is in Europe. Siberia is in Asia. You can drive from Europe to Asia in Russia without crossing any border or body of water.

questions.question: What is the name of the process by which the nucleus of an atom splits, releasing enormous amounts of energy?
questions.explanation: Nuclear fission occurs when a heavy atomic nucleus (such as uranium-235 or plutonium-239) absorbs a neutron and splits into two smaller nuclei, releasing additional neutrons and enormous amounts of energy. These released neutrons can then trigger further fissions — creating a chain reaction. This is the principle behind both nuclear reactors (controlled fission) and atomic bombs (uncontrolled fission).
questions.memory_hook: Fission = atom splitting. Fusion = atoms joining. Fission powers nuclear reactors and atomic bombs. Fusion powers the Sun (and hopefully future fusion reactors). Fusion releases more energy but is much harder to control. Fission we figured out in the 1940s. Fusion has been '30 years away' for 70 years.

questions.question: The Canary Islands, a Spanish autonomous community, are located off the coast of which African country — despite being geographically closer to Africa than to mainland Spain?
questions.explanation: The Canary Islands are located in the North Atlantic Ocean, about 100 km west of the southern Moroccan coast (near Western Sahara). Despite their African proximity, they have been Spanish territory since the 15th century. The seven main islands (Tenerife, Gran Canaria, Lanzarote, Fuerteventura, La Palma, La Gomera, El Hierro) have a subtropical climate and are one of Spain's top tourist destinations. Tenerife's Teide volcano (3,715 m) is Spain's highest peak.
questions.memory_hook: Canary Islands: Spanish territory 100 km off Morocco's coast. African climate, European passport. Seven islands, year-round tourism. Teide volcano (3,715m) is Spain's highest peak. Named not after canary birds but after 'Canariae Insulae' (island of dogs) — ancient Romans found large dogs there.

questions.question: What is centripetal force, and what provides it when a car turns a corner?
options: The outward force felt by passengers in a turn
An inward force toward the center of the circular path, provided by friction in a car turn
A force equal to gravity acting on moving objects
The force that causes objects to spin
questions.answer: An inward force toward the center of the circular path, provided by friction in a car turn
questions.explanation: Centripetal force is the force directed toward the center of a circular path that keeps an object moving in a curve rather than in a straight line. For a car turning, friction between the tires and road provides centripetal force. For Earth orbiting the Sun, gravity provides it. For a ball on a string, the string tension provides it. The 'centrifugal force' (feeling pushed outward in a turn) is not a real force — it is the inertial tendency of your body to continue moving straight.
questions.memory_hook: Centripetal force = inward force keeping circular motion. For a car turning: friction from tires. For Earth orbiting: gravity. For a ball on a string: string tension. 'Centrifugal force' (feeling pushed outward) isn't real — it's your inertia trying to go straight while the car turns.
